column7: Planar gastric emptying scintigraphy. Based on the images, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column8:
Gastroparesis
Mechanical gastric outlet obstruction
Medication-related delayed emptying
column9:
Gastroparesis: delayed emptying without mechanical obstruction.
Mechanical obstruction: requires structural correlation because scintigraphy alone demonstrates retention but not the obstructing lesion.
Medication effect: correlate with promotility-suppressing medications and whether they were appropriately held.

column3: Anterior-posterior planar images show an abnormal dual-phase gastric emptying scan. Emptying of both phases is abnormally rapid, reflective of dumping: Liquid (76% at 1 hour, 94% at 2 hours), solid (77% at 1 hour, 98% at 2 hours). Note no activity in stomach at 2 hours .
column7: Planar gastric emptying scintigraphy. Based on the images, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column8:
Dumping syndrome
Time-zero undercount from pre-imaging emptying
ROI/processing error
column9:
Dumping syndrome: genuinely accelerated liquid and solid transit.
Time-zero undercount: activity may already have left the stomach before the first image; recalculate relative to total abdominal counts.
ROI/processing error: verify complete gastric ROI and bowel exclusion.

column8:
Isolated liquid-phase delay
Isolated solid-phase delay
Concordant dual-phase delay
column9:
Isolated liquid delay: abnormal liquid curve with normal solid emptying.
Isolated solid delay: delayed solid emptying despite potentially normal liquid emptying.
Concordant delay: both phases show abnormal retention; the source emphasizes that the phases are not interchangeable.

column15: Define gastroparesis.

Boundary: It is a motility disorder, not retention caused by a fixed mechanical blockage.

column11: Why can liquid and solid gastric emptying be discordant?
column12:
Liquid emptying depends mainly on fundal accommodation and tonic fundal contraction, whereas solid emptying requires antral peristalsis, trituration, and pyloric passage of particles approximately 1 mm or smaller.
Because these phases depend on different motor functions, one may be abnormal while the other remains normal; the scintigraphic curves must therefore be interpreted separately.

column11: Why is the geometric mean preferred for calculating retained gastric activity in larger patients?
column12:
RadPrimer states that larger patients need dual-head imaging with geometric-mean calculation, and that geometric mean is generally the most appropriate method to calculate retained activity/emptying.
Outside clarification: Combining anterior and posterior counts as a geometric mean reduces projection-dependent attenuation bias as activity changes depth within the abdomen.

column11: Why can a time-zero image underestimate the true starting gastric activity in rapid emptying?
column12: If transit begins before the first acquired image, part of the meal has already entered bowel and is excluded from a stomach-only starting ROI. The apparent baseline gastric count is therefore too low. The source recommends considering total abdominal counts (stomach plus bowel) as the reference when this occurs.

column13: What is the pitfall of stopping an infant milk study after 1 hour, and what prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Assuming the 1-hour emptying value reliably predicts the 2-hour result.
Safety check: Continue imaging for the full 2 hours.
Discriminator: The source states that 1-hour emptying is a poor predictor of 2-hour emptying.
Why it happens: Early and later emptying rates are not interchangeable.

column13: What is the pitfall when the 2-hour value is abnormal but the 4-hour value is normal, and what prevents overcalling?
column14:
Trap: Labeling the study definitively abnormal from the 2-hour value alone.
Safety check: Review the completed 4-hour result and apply the institution's stated interpretive policy.
Discriminator: A normal 4-hour result may conflict with an abnormal 2-hour value.
Why it happens: Pediatric criteria and institutional interpretation are variable.

column13: What is the pitfall of using only one projection in a larger patient, and what prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Using single-projection counts in a larger patient when the source recommends dual-head acquisition with geometric-mean calculation.
Safety check: Use dual-head acquisition and geometric mean in young adults and larger patients.
Outside clarification: A single projection is more vulnerable to attenuation changes as gastric activity changes position/depth.

  • Gastroparesis is delayed gastric emptying without mechanical obstruction; dumping is abnormally rapid gastric emptying.
  • Solid gastric emptying scintigraphy is the diagnostic test of choice for suspected gastroparesis.
  • Milk studies in infants can assess reflux frequency/severity and gastric emptying, but normal values are sparse and formula-dependent.
  • Liquid, solid, and milk/formula emptying can be discordant; interpret each phase and protocol with its own limitations.
  • ROI placement, timing, decay correction, and geometric-mean processing are major accuracy checks.

column15: What mechanisms can cause gastroesophageal reflux in the RadPrimer differential for gastric motility studies?
column16: Increased duration/frequency of lower esophageal sphincter relaxation; compromised esophageal clearance; sphincter hypotonia; increased gastric acidity; and delayed gastric emptying.

column15: What symptoms are listed for gastroparesis?
column16:
Nausea, vomiting, early satiety, bloating, abdominal pain, and weight loss.
In infants, failure to thrive and irritability may be seen.

column15: What treatment options are listed for more severe or persistent gastroparesis?
column16:
Medical/diet options include proton pump inhibitors, promotility medications, small frequent meals, decreased fat, and lactose-free diet.
Severe weight loss or failure to thrive may require gastrostomy/gastrojejunostomy nutritional support; pyloroplasty and fundoplication are listed surgical options.

column15: How is an infant milk study acquired when assessing reflux versus gastric emptying?
column16:
Reflux assessment: anterior or posterior single-head images every 5-30 seconds for 1 hour to assess number and proximal severity of reflux events.
Gastric emptying assessment: anterior or posterior single-head images at time zero, 1 hour, and 2 hours, corrected for decay.

column15: How should nonstandard formulas or meals be reported when normal values are unavailable?
column16: Percentage emptying or half-time can still help clinical decision making, but the report should acknowledge that defined normal values may not exist for that specific formula or meal.
